issues_found: H2 pilot results (70.9% absorption reduction, 8x MSE) directly contradicted by full-scale results (TopK INCREASES absorption by 4.5x). Pilot uses Chanin first-letter probe; full-scale uses Gini absorption. The h2_pass flag checks wrong direction.

H5 'marginal fail at 5% threshold' uses a non-existent threshold. Actual pass criterion was >8% (computed from pilot). Achieved: 2.51%. Shortfall is 5.49pp. The 5% threshold was retroactively created and not in the experimental design.

Null controls use alpha=5 only; main H3 aggregates across [1,3,5,10,20]. At alpha=5: high-absorption=0.7485, low-absorption=0.7543, p=0.94 (statistically identical). Main H3 shows 18% difference. Paper claims effect is 'driven by high-alpha conditions' without pre-registration.

Feature selection threshold documented incorrectly. Paper states '50 high-absorption (UAS > 1.0)' but actual selected features have mean UAS ~0.57, well below 1.0. The documented threshold does not match the actual feature set.

H1 layer-wise absorption pattern is inconsistent across three experimental runs: pilot (layer 8 > layer 4), full H1 pilot (layer 8 > layer 4), full H1 GPT-2 atlas (monotonic DECREASE, peak at layer 2). Contradictory orderings.

Full H4 was run in PILOT mode (3 pairs), not full-scale, yet presented as 'full' results. Spearman r declined from pilot (0.8147) to full-pilot (0.587-0.706). The paper does not acknowledge this regression or explain why full-scale H4 was not completed.

Entanglement hypothesis is presented as a named contribution without empirical support beyond the H3 Spearman r=+0.35 correlation. No network analysis performed; 'hub feature' analogy introduced without evidence.
Section 4.2 says '~15% higher' but 0.1035/0.0874 = 18.4%. Abstract correctly says '18%'. Text underestimates the effect by ~3.4pp.

Abstract says absorption 'may indicate features at high leverage points... causally important' while Section 5 says 'computationally important' and Section 6 says 'deeply integrated.' Steering sensitivity does not directly measure causal importance; causal ablation studies are required.

Title and abstract promise multi-model scope (GPT-2 + Gemma-2B) and multi-layer atlas, but Gemma-2B experiments were skipped (HF authentication) and H1 layer-wise pattern is unresolved. Paper covers only GPT-2 Small layer 8.

issues_found: H2 pilot (Chanin first-letter probe: vanilla=0.225, TopK=0.066, 70.9% reduction) and full-scale (Gini absorption: vanilla=0.015, TopK=0.068, 4.5x INCREASE) use incompatible absorption metrics. Every mitigation variant (JumpReLU, OrtSAE, ATM) has HIGHER absorption than vanilla in full-scale. Paper presents these without labeling which metric produced which numbers.

H5 paper invents a 5% threshold: '4.95% vs 5% threshold, not significant (p=0.42).' Actual pass criterion was >8% (computed from pilot AUCs). Achieved causal delta: 2.51%. This is a 5.49pp shortfall, not a 'marginal miss.'

Null controls use alpha=5 only while main H3 aggregates across [1,3,5,10,20]. At alpha=5: high-absorption=0.7485, low-absorption=0.7543, p=0.94 (statistically identical). Main H3 18.4% difference is driven by high-alpha (10, 20). Paper claims 'driven by high-alpha conditions' -- post-hoc explanation for an internal contradiction.

OrtSAE ablation compares without-penalty (0.230 at L0~920) against with-penalty (0.247 at L0~550) to conclude 'orthogonality penalty does not appear to reduce absorption.' Comparison is at unmatched L0 -- the very confound the paper criticizes. Self-contradictory ablation design.

Probe quality confound in CMI analysis: absorption rate correlates with probe F1 at rho=-0.67 (p<0.001). CMI-absorption analysis uses L0=82 where mean probe F1=0.817. Low-CMI letters may be harder to probe, causing both low CMI and artificially high absorption rates. Paper never computes partial correlation controlling for probe F1.

CMI-absorption correlation reverses sign across dimensions: d'=10: rho=-0.383, d'=20: rho=+0.048, d'=30: rho=+0.299, d'=50: rho=+0.197. Bonferroni-corrected p=0.236. Sign reversal is qualitative failure, not sensitivity issue. Paper cherry-picks d'=10 as primary dimension without pre-specification.

Entanglement hypothesis presented as named contribution without empirical support beyond H3 Spearman r=+0.35. 'Hub feature' analogy introduced without network centrality analysis. Abstract claims 'causally important features' -- steering sensitivity does not measure causal importance (would require ablation studies).

The n=4 sparsity-absorption correlation (r~+0.93) has two points at identical L0=50. Driven by only 3 unique L0 values. Presented as 'striking relationship' and one of four contributions. Bootstrap CI does not address sample-size limitation.

MCC~0.21 across ALL variants including Random control suggests Hungarian matching yields chance-level recovery regardless of training. Paper acknowledges MCC failure but does not address implication: absorption reduction may reflect sparsity-induced suppression rather than genuine hierarchical feature recovery.
Section 3.5 promises one-way ANOVA across all completed variants but this is not reported in Results. Unfulfilled promise undermines credibility.

issues_found: Null controls use beta=5 only while main H3 aggregates [1,3,5,10,20]. At beta=5: high-absorption (0.7485) = low-absorption (0.7543), p=0.94. The absorption-sensitivity relationship is driven by beta=10 and beta=20. Null controls contradict, not validate, the main finding.

The entanglement hypothesis (Section 5) is pure post-hoc speculation with zero mechanistic evidence. r=+0.35 is a correlation, not a mechanism. No ablation, activation patching, gradient attribution, or feature suppression was conducted. Iteration 1 lessons explicitly warned about this.

Abstract and title use causal language ('more Causal', 'are More Causal') without causal ablation evidence. Steering sensitivity (max absolute logit difference) is a perturbation association measure, not causal importance. No experiment removes or suppresses a feature and measures behavioral change. This was flagged in iteration 1 reflections and remains completely unfixed.

Effect-size numbers are inconsistent throughout the paper: Section 4.2 says 'Effect ratio (high/low) = 1.18' AND 'Effect difference = 17.9%' while full_h3_summary.md shows 'Sensitivity Ratio (Low/High) = 0.84' (the inverse). Three different numbers for the same comparison. Iteration 1 lessons flagged this.

H5 downstream task evaluation achieved 2.51% causal delta against an intended >8% pass criterion. AUC values below 0.7 for both conditions suggest features may not reliably encode target concepts. Paper omits this history and presents 2.51% as acceptable. Iteration 1 lessons warned about post-hoc threshold fabrication.
All H2 mitigation variants (TopK, MultiScale, OrtSAE) show HIGHER absorption than vanilla SAE — negative results. Paper's framing is misleading.
Abstract and introduction imply multi-model scope (GPT-2 + Gemma-2B) but all experiments use only GPT-2 Small layer 8.
Absorption-sensitivity relationship is beta-conditional: effect emerges only at high steering magnitudes (beta=10, 20). At beta=5 (median), zero difference.
Symbol collision: beta for steering coefficient (Section 2.3) and UAS weight (Section 3.1). Alpha has the same collision. Unfixed despite being in action_items.json.
Paper outline planned 4 figures and 3 tables. Paper has zero figures beyond ASCII diagram and zero results tables.

issues_found: Null controls use beta=5 only vs main H3 aggregates [1,3,5,10,20]. At matched beta=5, high-absorption (0.7485) = low-absorption (0.7543), p=0.94 -- Table 2 directly contradicts the main claim. This has been documented as critical since iteration 1 and remains completely unfixed after 4 additional iterations.

Causal language throughout title/abstract/body (title: 'More Causal', abstract: 'more CAUSAL', Section 5: 'more causally relevant', Section 6.3: 'causal delta', conclusion: 'causally important') without any causal ablation evidence. The paper measures steering sensitivity (association), not causal contribution (ablation).

Probe quality confound invalidates CMI-based absorption measurement. Absorption rate correlates with probe F1 at rho=-0.67 (p<0.001). CMI analysis uses L0=82 where mean probe F1=0.817. Low-CMI letters may be harder to probe, causing both low CMI and artificially high absorption rates. Paper never computes partial correlation controlling for probe F1.

OrtSAE ablation compares unmatched L0 values (without penalty: L0~920 vs with penalty: L0~550) to conclude orthogonality penalty does not reduce absorption. This is the very confound the paper criticizes in architecture comparisons. The ablation conclusion is self-contradicting.

CMI-absorption correlation sign reversal across dimensions. At d'=10: rho=-0.383 (p=0.059 uncorrected). At d'=20+: rho=+0.048 to +0.299. The correlation changes sign depending on the dimension parameter -- qualitative failure.

MCC~0.21 across ALL variants including random control suggests Hungarian matching on overcomplete dictionaries yields chance-level recovery regardless of training. If SAEs do not recover ground-truth features, what does 'absorption reduction' mean?
Entanglement hypothesis (Section 5) is post-hoc speculation without mechanistic evidence. r=+0.35 correlation does not establish mechanism. No ablation, path-tracing, or activation patching.
Beta-conditional effect not prominently caveated. Effect disappears at beta=5 (median steering magnitude). Only emerges at beta=10 and beta=20. Most steering applications use moderate beta values.

Three different first-letter L24 16k absorption rates coexist: 27.1% (iter_009), 21.6% (iter_010 control), 34.5% (per-unique-word). The 21.6% control falls BELOW Table 2's CI [26.3%, 34.7%]. Contradictory numbers for same experiment.
References contain placeholder text 'Chanin, D. and et al.', 'Cunningham, H. and et al.' Unprofessional for conference submission. Cunningham et al. (ICLR 2024 Open Review) not yet published.

issues_found: The paper reports two contradictory empirical results without reconciliation: (1) original analysis: r=+0.35, p<0.001 under 'different methodology', and (2) controlled matched design: p=0.299 (aggregated null). The relationship between these two analyses is completely unexplained. Did the original methodology introduce confounds (activation frequency, decoder L2 norm) that produced a spurious positive correlation? The paper leaves readers to guess.

The beta=20 finding (low-absorption significantly MORE steerable than high-absorption, p=0.015) contradicts both the overall null result and the hypothesis framing. The abstract claims 'no significant difference across all steering magnitudes' but Table 1/2 shows a significant difference in the OPPOSITE direction at beta=20. With 5 beta comparisons, Bonferroni-corrected threshold is p<0.01; p=0.015 is NOT significant after correction.

The entanglement hypothesis (Section 5) was designed to explain WHY high-absorption features would be more steerable (r=+0.35 finding). Since that finding has been abandoned in favor of a null result, the hypothesis now has zero empirical grounding. A hypothesis without any supporting evidence for the main effect it was meant to explain is pure speculation. Now doubly orphaned (no positive finding, no mechanism experiments).

Two canonical paper versions exist in contradictory states: paper/paper.md (markdown) and writing/latex/main.tex (LaTeX). Different titles, different central findings, different random baseline numbers at beta=5 (0.7463 vs 0.6207), different steering coefficient symbols (alpha vs beta). Flagged in previous round and remains unfixed.

The paper has zero figures. Figure directories (writing/figures/ and writing/latex/figures/) are empty. For a NeurIPS/ICLR submission, a scatter plot of UAS vs steering sensitivity and a grouped bar chart by beta value are essential. Tables alone cannot convey the distribution of steering effects across 200 features. Risk of desk rejection.

The beta=20 reversal (low-absorption > high-absorption) may reflect saturation: high-absorption features have higher decoder L2 norms by construction, so at high steering magnitudes they may saturate the residual stream faster. The paper does not discuss this confound, which is a natural and likely explanation for the directional reversal.

Sections 4.4 (H2 Mitigation) and 4.5 (H5 Downstream) are labeled 'Pilot Evaluation' in text but appear as peer sections to H3 in the Experiments section without structural distinction. Readers cannot assess evidence strength without a preliminary/exploratory subsection.

References contain placeholder text: 'Chanin, D. and et al.', 'Cunningham, H. and et al.', 'Tian, Y. and et al.', 'Subramani, S. and Wang, Z. and others'. Cunningham et al. (ICLR 2024 Open Review) is not a published proceeding. This is unprofessional for a conference submission.

UAS hyperparameters (alpha=1.0, beta=0.5) are said to be from 'validation experiments' with no details. What was validated? What search space was explored? What validation set was used? No trivial baselines compared (activation frequency alone, L2 decoder norm alone, random).
The 10 test prompts are not listed or provided. Steering sensitivity is prompt-dependent; readers need access to the specific prompts to reproduce or verify results.
issues_fixed: Null controls now use full beta range [1,3,5,10,20] matching main H3 protocol — critical methodological flaw from prior iterations is resolved
Causal language removed from title and throughout the paper — 'More Causal' and 'are More Causal' replaced with neutral framing
Beta=5 only null control (p=0.94) no longer contradicts main finding — now correctly validates the null at all steering magnitudes

issues_found: The paper presents two contradictory empirical results without reconciling them: (1) original analysis: r=+0.35, p<0.001, and (2) controlled matched design: p=0.299. The 'different methodology' note in Section 4.2 does not explain WHAT changed and WHY the result changed. The critical question is unanswered: did the original methodology introduce confounds (activation frequency, decoder L2 norm) that produced a spurious positive correlation?

The beta=20 finding (low-absorption > high-absorption, p=0.015) contradicts both the overall null result and the hypothesis framing. The title/abstract claim 'no significant difference across all steering magnitudes' is factually incorrect. With 5 beta comparisons, Bonferroni-corrected threshold is p<0.01; p=0.015 does NOT survive correction.

The entanglement hypothesis (Section 5) was designed to explain the r=+0.35 finding, which has been abandoned. Since that positive finding has been replaced by a null result, the hypothesis now has zero empirical grounding. A hypothesis without any supporting evidence for the main effect it was meant to explain is pure speculation. Now doubly orphaned: no positive finding to explain AND no mechanism experiments.

The beta=20 reversal (low-absorption > high-absorption) may reflect saturation: high-absorption features have higher decoder L2 norms by construction, so at high steering magnitudes they may saturate the residual stream faster. The paper does not discuss this natural explanation.

Two contradictory paper versions persist: paper/paper.md and writing/latex/main.tex. The two versions differ in title, abstract, tables, and steering coefficient symbol (alpha vs beta). This was flagged in iteration 3 review and remains unfixed.

Zero figures exist despite NeurIPS/ICLR expectations. Both writing/figures/ and writing/latex/figures/ directories are empty. Tables alone cannot convey the distribution of 200 features or the beta-conditional pattern. Desk-rejection risk.
Sections 4.4 (H2 Mitigation) and 4.5 (H5 Downstream) are labeled 'Pilot Evaluation' in text but appear as peer sections to H3 in the Experiments section without structural distinction.
References contain placeholder text: 'Chanin, D. and et al.', 'Cunningham, H. and et al.', 'Tian, Y. and et al.', 'Subramani, S. and Wang, Z. and others'. Unprofessional for conference submission.
The paper tests absorption significance at each of 5 beta values but does not apply multiple comparison correction. At beta=20, p=0.015 is NOT significant after Bonferroni correction.
The 10 test prompts are not listed or provided. Steering sensitivity is prompt-dependent; readers need access to the specific prompts to reproduce or verify results.
issues_fixed: Causal language removed throughout the paper (previously 'More Causal' framing) — critical issue from prior iterations resolved
Null controls now use full beta range [1,3,5,10,20] instead of only beta=10 — critical methodological flaw from prior iterations resolved
Internal consistency of null result achieved: both markdown and LaTeX versions now report p=0.299
